mechanism: Collect binary responses from a population of survey respondents to vignettes describing agents in specified scenarios. Compute empirical proportions of affirmative responses across participant groups (philosophers vs. non-philosophers, internalists vs. externalists). Test whether observed proportions in replication studies differ from published baseline proportions using significance tests. Compare magnitude and consistency of reported effects to identify potential confounds in the original experimental design.

Collect Likert-scale responses from an entity population (clients, N=170 nested within therapists, N=42) on a newly developed measurement instrument. Decompose the response matrix using dimensionality reduction (factor analysis) to identify latent factors. Compute internal consistency coefficients for each identified factor. Quantify associations between factor scores and outcome variables using correlation coefficients in bivariate analysis and random-intercept regression models in multivariate analysis.

Manually classify text content (newspaper articles, television broadcasts, political advertisements) into thematic categories to construct multi-sourced agenda measures (vectors of issue salience across sources). Quantify temporal cross-lag associations between agenda vectors from different sources at sequential time-points using cross-lagged correlation coefficients. Statistical inference identifies which source agendas predict subsequent agendas from competing sources.

Present visual stimuli and record binary responses (correct/incorrect recognition or matching) in a two-alternative forced-choice task. Measure the difference between accuracy when complete stimuli are presented versus when parts are presented in isolation (complete-over-part advantage). Compare this measure across stimulus types (faces vs. houses) and task variations (recognition vs. matching) to characterize the degree of holistic integration. Test whether the effect persists across probe delays and experimental conditions.

Review empirical studies investigating how moral or evaluative judgments influence lay attributions of intentionality to agents performing actions. Model the bias using an affect-based attribution framework that predicts intentionality ascriptions increase when an action is immoral and blameworthy. Apply this theoretical mechanism to reasoning about jury decision-making, where mens rea (guilty intent) determinations could be biased by the immoral nature of a criminal act.

Present words in a flanker task where a central target word is flanked by two identical peripheral words (flankers) and measure reaction time and accuracy of lexical decision responses to the target. Manipulate the morphological relationship between target and flanker (transparent suffixation, pseudo-suffixation, no morphological relation) as a within-subject factor. Estimate the facilitation effect of morphologically transparent flankers relative to orthographically similar but non-transparent flankers using response-latency differences.

Partition participants into experimental groups varying a single factor: the numerical range of response categories in a survey scale. Collect self-reported behavioral data (television watching duration) using the assigned scale. Compute descriptive statistics (mean reported usage, rating scale interpretation). Test whether the median or range of the scale influenced reported behavior and subsequent judgments using between-group comparisons. Infer anchoring effects from differences in reported behavior and inferred social averages.

Conduct factorial experiments varying the order and context of survey questions: specific versus general questions, presented as integrated or distinct conversational units, with multiple configurations of question sequences. Measure the magnitude of assimilation versus contrast effects in general judgments as a function of prior specific-question exposure. Develop a theoretical model predicting when shared conversational context triggers redundancy norms that reverse information-reuse from assimilation to contrast.

Conduct a series of between-subjects experiments where participants view multimedia sequences (television programs with or without embedded interruptions) and report enjoyment ratings. Test multiple mechanistic hypotheses: (1) does interruption improve enjoyment universally; (2) does the quality of interrupting content matter; (3) does mere presence of pauses suffice; (4) does interrupt type vary the effect. Identify boundary conditions by testing on populations with varying adaptation propensity and program content varying in inherent adaptation potential.

Assemble balanced panel data of multiple countries (N=18 OECD) over time (1970-2003) with measurements of employment in manufacturing, consumer expenditure patterns, sectoral productivity growth, and trade-flow metrics. Estimate a linear regression model with country-level and time-period fixed effects, regressing manufacturing employment share on three explanatory factors: consumer affluence, relative productivity growth, and trade intensity. Partition effects into direct and indirect channels via mediation analysis.

Acquire administrative or survey data linking wage levels, firm size, occupational classification, and span-of-control measures (number of subordinates) for entities (workers). Stratify by job responsibility (supervisory vs. nonsupervisory). Within strata, compute wage differentials attributable to firm size and estimate how these differentials scale with responsibility level. Test the hypothesis that larger firms' wage premium increases steeply with managerial rank, consistent with models where larger organizational size amplifies returns to managerial talent.

Conduct experiments where participants observe visual patterns (dots or geometric shapes) arranged either homogeneously (all one color or type) or heterogeneously (multiple colors or types), with numerosity held constant. Request quantity judgments and compare estimates between pattern types. Measure the effect of variety on perceived magnitude. Test boundary conditions: whether the effect depends on the number of distinct types and whether making area salient via framing removes the effect.

Design factorial experiments crossing two factors: source expertise (low vs. high) and source certainty (high confidence vs. low confidence statements). Expose entities to persuasive messages from sources varying on these dimensions. Measure elaboration/involvement and persuasion outcomes. Test the interaction: low-expertise sources violate expectations and increase persuasion when expressing certainty; high-expertise sources violate expectations and increase persuasion when expressing uncertainty.

Measure individual differences in system confidence (belief that the current social system is legitimate). Experimentally manipulate system threat (verbal attack vs. control). Observe choice behavior across consumption options that vary in how directly they defend the system (explicit vs. implicit defense opportunities). Predict differential choice patterns as a function of confidence-by-threat interaction: low-confidence individuals select implicit defense; high-confidence individuals select explicit defense.

Manipulate visual depictions of products in advertisements (object orientation relative to viewer's dominant hand to facilitate mental simulation). Measure purchase intentions and infer the mechanism by testing whether manipulating perceptual resources (occupying motor simulation capacity) attenuates the effect. Repeat across valence conditions (positive vs. negative products). Estimate effect sizes and boundary conditions to support an embodied simulation account.

Exploit a cultural cognitive structure: readers of left-to-right languages mentally represent temporal progression with past on the left and future on the right. Vary horizontal position of product images in advertisements (left vs. right) and measure attitudes. Hypothesize congruence between spatial position and implicit temporal content enhances processing fluency and attitude favorability. Test moderation by need for structure and reversal in right-to-left readers.

Decompose product value into high-level (desirability, why) and low-level (feasibility, how) feature dimensions. Measure willingness-to-sell and willingness-to-buy price for objects whose high- and low-level feature values vary independently. Compare price differentials as a function of feature alignment. Test mediation via construal level (sellers construe at higher level than buyers). Identify conditions where selling prices exceed buying prices based on high-level feature superiority.

Manipulate resource scarcity (perceived constraint: tight vs. abundant) and observe consumption-planning strategies in surveys and field studies. Categorize planning responses into two types: efficiency (stretching the resource via economical allocation) and prioritization (sacrificing lower-priority goals). Measure the relative cost perception, likelihood, and effectiveness of each strategy as constraint varies. Test behavioral outcomes (impulsive spending, overspending, savings goal achievement) to identify dysfunctional adaptation patterns.

Assign entities to experimental conditions varying the presence of a personal smartphone (own device present vs. absent). Measure available cognitive capacity using performance-based tasks. Quantify cognitive costs as the decrement in task performance or working-memory availability attributable to smartphone presence. Estimate heterogeneity in effect magnitude by measuring individual differences in smartphone dependence and testing interaction with condition.

Acquire large-scale survey data on new business formation rates and population demographics (median age) across many entities (countries). Estimate the relationship between median age and entrepreneurship rate via regression. Quantify age-related heterogeneity: compute how entrepreneurship varies with cohort age within each country after controlling for aggregate age distribution. Synthesize findings into a theoretical model predicting that skill concentration in senior cohorts blocks younger cohort opportunity access.
